title: Prompting: How to Actually Talk to AI
subtitle: Simple, practical skills to get great answers
theme: Turn the nervous "am I asking this right?" feeling into quiet confidence, so a total beginner walks away knowing they can get great answers from any AI just by talking to it well.
learningObjectives: Explain why being clear and giving context makes an AI's answers dramatically better
Use a real example inside your request to show the AI exactly what you want
Ask the AI to work step by step and to take on a helpful role
Refine a weak answer through a short back-and-forth instead of giving up
Recognize and avoid the most common beginner prompting mistakes
storyHook: Yesterday you watched AI learn to chat with us like a friend across a table. But here's the quiet secret nobody tells beginners: the same AI can give one person a brilliant, useful answer and give another person mush, even when they ask about the exact same thing. The difference isn't luck, and it isn't a magic password. It's simply how you ask. Today you learn that skill, and it's easier than ordering coffee.

slides.title: Welcome: The Skill Hiding in Plain Sight
slides.body:
Welcome back. Over the last ten days you've watched something amazing grow up: from the tiny idea of *attention* (the AI's way of deciding which words matter most), to giant models trained on the world's text, to the chat window you can now talk to like a friend.

Today we get practical. We're going to learn *prompting* — and a prompt is just the message you type to an AI. That's it. No code, no secret commands.

Here's why today matters. Think of an AI as an incredibly knowledgeable, eager new assistant who started this morning. They know a staggering amount, but they can't read your mind. If you mumble, they guess. If you're clear, they shine.

The good news: getting great answers isn't about being clever or technical. It's about being clear, the same way you'd be clear with a helpful stranger. By the end of today you'll have six simple habits that turn vague replies into genuinely useful ones. Let's begin.
slides.speakerNotes: Set a warm, reassuring tone. Emphasize that prompting is a learnable everyday skill, not a technical one.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, 'prompting' shows up on resumes, in classrooms, and in everyday work — people who ask well simply get more out of the same tools everyone else has.

slides.title: Habit 1: Be Clear and Give Context
slides.body:
Imagine texting a friend just the word "thoughts?" with no other information. They'd reply, "On what?" The AI is the same. The single biggest upgrade you can make is to add *context* — the background details that tell the AI what's really going on.

Compare two ways of asking. Vague: "Write an email." The AI has to guess everything. Clear: "Write a short, friendly email to my landlord asking to fix a leaking tap, mentioning it's been dripping for three days." Same effort to type, wildly better result.

Think of it like ordering at a restaurant. "Food, please" gets you a confused waiter. "A medium veggie pizza, no onions, extra cheese" gets you exactly what you wanted.

A simple recipe to remember: say *who* it's for, *what* you want, and *why* or in what situation. You don't need fancy words — plain language is perfect. The AI isn't judging your grammar. It just needs enough to picture the task.

Whenever an answer disappoints you, the first question to ask yourself is: did I give it enough context? Usually the answer is no.
slides.speakerNotes: Have learners mentally rewrite a vague prompt into a context-rich one. This is the highest-leverage habit.
slides.marketConnection: Customer support teams, teachers, and marketers in 2026 keep short 'context templates' so every AI request starts with the right background baked in.

slides.title: A Tale of Two Requests
slides.body:
Let me tell you about two people who asked an AI the very same thing on the same afternoon.

Priya typed: "Give me ideas for my mom's birthday." The AI offered a generic list — a cake, flowers, a card. Fine, but forgettable. Priya shrugged and closed the app.

Arjun typed: "Give me three birthday gift ideas for my mom. She's turning 60, loves gardening and old Hindi film music, and my budget is about 2,000 rupees. Keep them thoughtful, not generic." The AI suggested a set of heirloom seeds with a planter, a curated playlist printed as a pretty card with a small speaker, and a hand-labeled memory jar. Arjun grinned.

Same AI. Same minute. The only difference was that Arjun handed over the details that lived in his head — age, interests, budget, the feeling he wanted.

This is the whole game in one story. The AI is like a talented friend who's never met your mother. The more you tell it about her, the more it can help. You're not writing a perfect prompt; you're just sharing what you already know. Small details, big difference.
slides.speakerNotes: Use this story to make the abstract 'give context' rule emotionally memorable.
slides.marketConnection: This exact gap — vague versus detailed asks — is why two coworkers using the same AI tool in 2026 often get very different results.

slides.title: Habit 2: Show an Example of What You Want
slides.body:
Sometimes describing what you want is hard, but *showing* it is easy. This is one of the most powerful and underused tricks: give the AI a small example to copy.

Think about teaching someone to fold a paper boat. You could write three paragraphs of instructions, or you could fold one in front of them and say, "Like this." The second way is faster and clearer. Examples work the same magic for AI.

Suppose you want product descriptions in a certain style. Instead of explaining the tone, show one: "Here's a description I like: 'Cozy, hand-knit socks that feel like a warm hug for your feet.' Now write one in that same style for a wool scarf." The AI instantly catches the playful, sensory tone.

This works for almost anything — the format of a list, the length of a reply, the voice of an email, even how you want data laid out. One good example is worth a hundred words of description.

The phrase to remember is simple: "Here's an example of what I mean." Then paste it. The AI is a brilliant mimic, so give it something good to mimic.
slides.speakerNotes: Stress that examples replace hard-to-describe instructions. Encourage pasting a sample, not explaining a style.
slides.marketConnection: Writers and analysts in 2026 routinely paste one sample document and say 'match this format' to generate dozens more consistently.

slides.title: Habit 3: Ask It to Go Step by Step
slides.body:
When a question is big or has a few moving parts, add one small phrase: "Let's go step by step." It sounds almost too simple, but it genuinely changes the quality of the answer.

Here's why. Picture a friend solving a tricky math puzzle in their head versus working it out on paper. On paper, they catch their own mistakes, because each step has to make sense before the next one. Asking AI to slow down and show its work has the same effect — it tends to reason more carefully and skip fewer things.

For example, instead of "How do I plan a small wedding on a tight budget?" try "Help me plan a small wedding on a tight budget. Go step by step: first the must-haves, then where to save, then a rough timeline." You'll get an organized plan instead of a jumbled paragraph.

This also helps *you*. When the answer arrives in clear steps, it's easier to follow, easier to question, and easier to act on. If one step looks wrong, you can point at exactly that step and ask for a fix.

Big task? Break it into a walk, not a leap.
slides.speakerNotes: Note this previews Day 16 (reasoning models) gently — step-by-step thinking is a core idea.
slides.marketConnection: Step-by-step prompting is standard in 2026 for planning, troubleshooting, and tutoring, where people want reasoning they can follow and trust.

slides.title: Habit 4: Give It a Role to Play
slides.body:
Here's a fun and surprisingly effective trick: tell the AI *who to be*. This is called giving it a role, and it's like handing an actor a costume.

Watch the difference. "Explain investing" gets you a textbook answer. "You're a patient teacher explaining investing to a complete beginner who's a little nervous about money — keep it simple and kind" gets you something warmer, gentler, and far more useful for that situation.

Why does this work? A role packs a lot of instructions into a few words. Saying "act as a friendly fitness coach" quietly tells the AI the right tone, the right level of detail, and even the right encouragement, all at once. It's shorthand that the AI understands instantly, the way you'd picture a whole personality from the words "strict librarian" or "cheerful tour guide."

You can pick almost any helpful role: a careful editor, a curious interviewer, a no-nonsense travel planner, a supportive study buddy. Match the role to what you actually need.

Remember, the AI isn't really becoming that person — it's adjusting its style to fit the costume you gave it. But the effect on the answer is real and immediate.
slides.speakerNotes: Clarify the role is a style/perspective shortcut, not the AI 'pretending' to be conscious. Keep it light.
slides.marketConnection: Role prompts power many 2026 apps behind the scenes — the 'tutor', 'coach', or 'assistant' you chat with is often one big role instruction.

slides.title: Habit 5: Try Again and Refine
slides.body:
Here's the habit that separates frustrated beginners from confident users: when the first answer isn't quite right, *don't start over and don't give up.* Just talk back.

The biggest myth about prompting is that you need the perfect message on the first try. You don't. Real prompting is a conversation, like steering a friend toward what you meant. "That's close, but make it shorter." "Good, now make it sound less formal." "Can you add an example?" Each nudge gets you closer.

Think of it like adjusting the shower temperature. You rarely hit the perfect warmth on the first turn of the tap. You feel the water, tweak it a little, feel again. Two or three small adjustments and it's just right. Prompting is exactly that loop.

Because the AI remembers the conversation (as you saw on Day 10), you can build on what's already there instead of re-explaining everything. Short follow-ups do the trick: "more casual," "too long, cut it in half," "give me three options instead."

So expect to refine. It's not a sign you did it wrong — it's how everyone gets great results.
slides.speakerNotes: Reframe 'a bad first answer' as the normal start of a refining loop, not a failure. Connect back to Day 10 memory.
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, the people who get the most from AI treat it as a quick back-and-forth, refining in seconds rather than accepting the first draft.

slides.title: Habit 6: Common Beginner Mistakes to Avoid
slides.body:
You've learned what to do. Now let's quickly dodge the potholes most beginners hit.

*Being too vague.* "Help me with my business" gives the AI nothing to grab. Add the who, what, and why. It's the difference between "directions" and "directions to the train station from here."

*Asking ten things at once.* Cramming five unrelated questions into one message muddles the answer. Ask one clear thing, then the next. One dish at a time, not the whole buffet on one plate.

*Trusting blindly.* AI sounds confident even when it's wrong — it can "make things up," which experts call *hallucination*. For anything important — health, money, facts, dates — double-check with a trusted source. Treat the AI as a smart first draft, not the final word.

*Giving up after one weak answer.* Remember Habit 5: refine, don't quit.

*Forgetting it's not human.* It has no feelings to spare and no memory of you between separate chats unless told. Be clear rather than polite-but-fuzzy.

Avoid these five and you're already ahead of most people using AI today. None of them require skill — just awareness.
slides.speakerNotes: Hallucination gets its one-line definition here. Emphasize verifying important facts without scaring beginners off.
slides.marketConnection: Schools and workplaces in 2026 teach 'verify before you trust' as the basic safety rule for using AI responsibly.

slides.title: Recap: You Can Talk to AI Now
slides.body:
Take a breath — you just learned a real, practical skill. Let's gather it up.

Getting great answers from AI comes down to six friendly habits. *Be clear and give context* — tell it who, what, and why. *Show an example* when describing is hard; the AI is a brilliant mimic. *Ask it to go step by step* for anything big, so the thinking stays careful. *Give it a role* to instantly set the right tone. *Try again and refine* — treat it like adjusting a shower, not acing a test. And *avoid the common traps*: vagueness, asking too much at once, blind trust, and giving up early.

Notice that none of this was technical. It was really just good communication — the same clarity and back-and-forth you'd use with a helpful new colleague.

The best part? You can practice these for free, right now, in any chat window. Every conversation makes you a little sharper. Prompting isn't a talent you're born with; it's a habit you build, one message at a time.

You now know how to *talk* to AI. Tomorrow, things get visual.
slides.speakerNotes: End on encouragement and momentum. Reinforce that practice is free and immediate, then hand off to Day 12.
slides.marketConnection: By 2026, clear prompting is a quiet everyday superpower — the same tool gives far more to the person who knows these six habits.

keyTakeaways: A prompt is just the message you type — getting great answers is about clear communication, not technical skill
Context is king: tell the AI who it's for, what you want, and why, every single time
When words fall short, show an example — the AI copies a good sample better than it follows a long description
'Go step by step' and 'act as a [role]' are two tiny phrases that dramatically improve answers
Refining is normal, not failure — nudge the AI with short follow-ups like a conversation
Always double-check important facts, because a confident AI can still be wrong (hallucination)
marketSnapshot2026: By 2026, 'prompting' has quietly become a baseline life skill, like searching the web was twenty years ago. Job listings mention it casually, schools teach it alongside writing, and the gap between people who get mediocre AI results and brilliant ones often comes down to these few habits rather than which tool they pay for. Most modern AI apps also do invisible prompting for you behind the scenes — the 'tutor' or 'coach' persona you chat with is usually one well-crafted role instruction — which means understanding prompting also helps you understand how the apps around you actually work.
homework: Pick one real thing you want help with this week — a message, a plan, a decision, anything. Write your first prompt the lazy way (vague, one line) and see what you get. Then rewrite it using at least three of today's habits: add context, give an example, ask for steps, or assign a role. Compare the two answers side by side. Finally, do one round of refining ('shorter,' 'friendlier,' 'add an example') and notice how much better the result becomes. Bring your before-and-after to the next session.
nextDayTeaser: You've learned to talk to AI with words — but tomorrow, words start turning into pictures. In Day 12, 'AI That Draws: Pictures From Words,' you'll discover how typing a simple sentence can conjure an entirely new image that never existed before, and how the prompting habits you just learned apply to art too. Get ready to be amazed.
